---
title: "Multi-type branching processes"
subtitle: "Age-structured and heterogeneous transmission"
format:
  html:
    code-fold: false
engine: julia
---

## Overview

EpiBranch.jl supports multi-type branching processes where individuals
belong to types (age groups, risk groups, spatial patches) and
transmission between types is governed by an offspring matrix.

```{julia}
using EpiBranch
using Distributions
using DataFrames
using Random: Xoshiro
```

## Offspring matrix

The offspring matrix `M[i,j]` gives the expected number of type-`i`
offspring from a type-`j` parent. This is the next-generation matrix
from the branching process literature.

For infectious disease, you typically construct it from a contact matrix
× transmission probability × infectious duration.

```{julia}
# 3 age groups: children (0-14), adults (15-64), elderly (65+)
# Contact rates (contacts per day)
contacts = [8.0 3.0 1.0;    # children contact children, adults, elderly
            3.0 6.0 2.0;    # adults
            1.0 2.0 4.0]    # elderly

# Transmission probability per contact × mean infectious duration
q = 0.05  # probability per contact-day
D = 5.0   # mean infectious duration in days

# Next-generation matrix
M = contacts .* q .* D
println("Offspring matrix M:")
display(M)
println("\nType-specific R (column sums):")
println("  Children: ", sum(M[:, 1]))
println("  Adults:   ", sum(M[:, 2]))
println("  Elderly:  ", sum(M[:, 3]))
```

## Simulation with offspring matrix

Pass the matrix and a function that constructs the offspring distribution
for each type's R value:

```{julia}
model = BranchingProcess(
    M,
    R_j -> NegBin(R_j, 0.5),   # NegBin with type-specific mean, shared dispersion
    LogNormal(1.6, 0.5);        # generation time
    type_labels = ["0-14", "15-64", "65+"]
)

rng = Xoshiro(42)
state = simulate(model;
    sim_opts = SimOpts(max_cases = 500),
    rng = rng
)

# Count cases by type
infected = filter(is_infected, state.individuals)
for (i, label) in enumerate(["0-14", "15-64", "65+"])
    n = count(ind -> individual_type(ind) == i, infected)
    println("$label: $n cases")
end
```

## Assortative vs disassortative mixing

Assortative mixing (types mostly infect their own type):

```{julia}
M_assort = [3.0 0.1 0.1;
            0.1 3.0 0.1;
            0.1 0.1 3.0]

model_assort = BranchingProcess(M_assort, R_j -> Poisson(R_j), Exponential(5.0);
    type_labels = ["A", "B", "C"])

rng = Xoshiro(42)
state_a = simulate(model_assort;
    sim_opts = SimOpts(max_cases = 200),
    rng = rng
)

# Check: offspring should mostly be the same type as parent
same_type = 0
diff_type = 0
infected = filter(is_infected, state_a.individuals)
for ind in infected
    ind.parent_id == 0 && continue
    parent = state_a.individuals[findfirst(i -> i.id == ind.parent_id, state_a.individuals)]
    if individual_type(ind) == individual_type(parent)
        same_type += 1
    else
        diff_type += 1
    end
end
println("Same type: $same_type, different type: $diff_type")
println("Assortativity: $(round(same_type / (same_type + diff_type), digits=2))")
```

## Interventions with multi-type

Interventions work unchanged — they operate on individual state,
not types:

```{julia}
iso = Isolation(delay = Exponential(2.0))
ct = ContactTracing(probability = 0.5, delay = Exponential(1.0))

model = BranchingProcess(
    M,
    R_j -> NegBin(R_j, 0.5),
    LogNormal(1.6, 0.5)
)

rng = Xoshiro(42)
results = simulate_batch(model, 200;
    interventions = [iso, ct],
    sim_opts = SimOpts(
        max_cases = 500,
        incubation_period = LogNormal(1.5, 0.5),
    ),
    rng = rng
)

cp = containment_probability(results)
println("Containment probability with age structure: ", round(cp, digits = 3))
```

## Custom offspring function

For full control, pass a function directly:

```{julia}
# Type 1 is a super-spreader type, type 2 is normal
function my_offspring(rng, parent_type)
    if parent_type == 1
        # Super-spreaders: high R, low k, mostly infect type 2
        n = rand(rng, NegBin(5.0, 0.1))
        type1 = rand(rng, Binomial(n, 0.2))  # 20% same type
        return [type1, n - type1]
    else
        # Normal: moderate R
        n = rand(rng, Poisson(1.5))
        type1 = rand(rng, Binomial(n, 0.1))  # 10% become super-spreaders
        return [type1, n - type1]
    end
end

model = BranchingProcess(my_offspring, Exponential(5.0);
    n_types = 2, type_labels = ["super-spreader", "normal"])

rng = Xoshiro(42)
state = simulate(model; sim_opts = SimOpts(max_cases = 200), rng = rng)

infected = filter(is_infected, state.individuals)
n_ss = count(ind -> individual_type(ind) == 1, infected)
n_normal = count(ind -> individual_type(ind) == 2, infected)
println("Super-spreaders: $n_ss, Normal: $n_normal")
```

## Chain statistics with multi-type

Chain statistics aggregate across types — they count total infected per chain:

```{julia}
cs = chain_statistics(state)
println("Chains: $(nrow(cs))")
println("Sizes: ", cs.size)
```

## Finite population with multi-type

Population depletion works with multi-type — currently at the aggregate level
(not per-type):

```{julia}
model = BranchingProcess(
    M,
    R_j -> Poisson(R_j),
    Exponential(5.0);
    population_size = 200
)

rng = Xoshiro(42)
state = simulate(model; sim_opts = SimOpts(max_cases = 500), rng = rng)
println("Cases in finite population: ", state.cumulative_cases, " / 200")
```
